project_overview.project_development_objective: To expand economic activity between Pakistan and Afghanistan by improving regional connectivity and promoting private sector development along the Khyber Pass corridor
project_overview.total_financing: US$ 460.6 million (SDR 320.3 million equivalent) IDA Credit, with US$ 22.15 million counterpart funding from Government of Pakistan
project_overview.beneficiaries: Road users, traders, consumers, SME owners and employees, internally-displaced persons, women, youth, and disadvantaged segments of society in Pakistan and Afghanistan; Afghan medical patients and students; consumers of tradeable goods and services along the Khyber Pass corridor

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - FATA is fragile and conflict-affected; 73% of FATA population lives in multidimensional poverty; security situation has improved but remains a risk; project involves Afghanistan which is FCV-affected

key_components.component_name: Component I: Expressway Development (Peshawar-Torkham Expressway - PTEX)
key_components.description: Construction of 48 km four-lane dual highway with improved geometry, fiber optic cables, land acquisition and resettlement, afforestation, and technical reviews. Includes 25 pre-stressed bridges, 2 interchanges, 2 flyovers, 8 subways/underpasses, 123 box culverts, and 2 cattle creeps. Climate-resilient design incorporating seismic, landslide, and flood mitigation measures.
key_components.financing: US$ 402.75 million (IDA: US$ 383.60 million; Counterpart: US$ 19.15 million)

key_components.component_name: Component II: Development of the Khyber Pass Economic Corridor
key_components.description: Two sub-components: (a) Technical Assistance (US$ 7 million) - Spatial Master Plan for WGP, feasibility studies, cultural heritage documentation, impact evaluation; (b) Infrastructure Investments (US$ 68 million) - secondary roads, urban development, logistics hub, industrial/commercial infrastructure, transportation terminals, cultural sites development, traffic management, with focus on women's participation and gender-sensitive amenities
key_components.financing: US$ 75 million (IDA: US$ 72 million; Counterpart: US$ 3 million)

key_components.description: Incremental operating costs and consultant support for project implementation, including PIU operations and Component II implementation support

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: National Highways Authority (NHA) as Project Implementation Entity; FATA Secretariat as owner of Component II; Economic Affairs Division (EAD) as borrower
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee chaired by NHA with representatives from EAD, Ministry of Communications, SAFRON, FATA Secretariat, and KP Government; Project Implementation Unit (PIU) in NHA's North Zone Office in Peshawar with representatives from NHA and FATA Secretariat; coordination with development partners (ADB, USAID, EU, KfW, UN, MDTF); bilateral mechanisms with Afghanistan (Joint Economic Commission, Joint Business Council)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Design and Build (D&B) approach for PTEX; World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised November 2017); STEP (Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement) system; competitive procurement for consulting services; bidding documents include Codes of Conduct on environment, health, safety, GBV, and SEA prevention
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Semi-annual implementation progress reviews; quarterly safeguard reporting; annual network-level surveys by NHA for travel time, vehicle operating costs, border crossing times, road fatalities; third-party monitoring after 6 months for quality assurance; impact evaluation for Component II; grievance redress mechanism monitoring; mid-term review at 36 months; monitoring of environmental and social management plans; tracking of gender indicators and women-owned firm participation

identified_risks.risk_description: Border skirmishes between Pakistan and Afghanistan; political instability; potential disruption to project implementation and operation; security risks in FATA despite recent improvements; upcoming national elections creating uncertainties
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Engagement of both governments; involvement of political administration in FATA and traditional Jirgas; involvement of local tribes in transport and trade; use of Frontier Constabulary (FC) - well-trained federal paramilitary force with no known negative legacy; FC personnel from the area with mandatory training on gender issues and GBV; coordination with development partners; recent improvement in bilateral relations between Pakistan and Afghanistan

identified_risks.risk_description: Deterioration of macroeconomic environment with worsening current account and fiscal deficits; declining external balances; overvalued rupee; potential restrictions on imports needed for project; deferred budgetary allocations; rising trade imbalance with Afghanistan; upcoming national elections
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Bank and development partners engaging separately with authorities to address macro risks; project design flexibility; counterpart funding commitment from GoP

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential policy inconsistencies; regulatory barriers to trade; administrative disjuncture between KP and FATA; institutional weaknesses
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design incorporates policy harmonization platform; leverages bilateral institutional mechanisms; APTTA and transit trade agreements in place; planned merger of FATA with KP over five years; complementary investments in trade facilitation

identified_risks.risk_description: Component II investments not yet fully defined; potential for delays in achieving PDO; complex terrain with seismic activity, landslides, and flash floods; need for specialized design expertise
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Many proposed interventions address identified constraints and are not dependent on masterplan completion; ongoing preparation of TORs for activities; climate-resilient design incorporating seismic zoning, hydrological calculations, slope stabilization, afforestation; road safety audits; experienced international consultants; D&B approach includes value for money analyses

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Need for coordination between NHA and FATA Secretariat; large scope of Component II requiring diverse skill sets; potential capacity gaps in PIU; sustainability of Component II infrastructure maintenance
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: NHA's demonstrated capacity in implementing large highway projects (US$ 8 billion in 77 contracts since 2012); PIU includes representatives from both NHA and FATA Secretariat; technical experts to support implementation; training and capacity building for PIU staff; NHA's road asset management system for maintenance; PPP modalities for Component II sustainability; local government and stakeholder involvement in maintenance

identified_risks.risk_description: Financial management system improvements needed; computerized accounting system upgrades required; potential for fraud and corruption in large procurement contracts
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: NHA's autonomous body status with professional financial management; previous successful World Bank project implementation; designated accounting section for foreign-funded projects; virtual private network linking regional offices; improved computerized accounting system planned; STEP system for transparency; Open Data Portal development; procurement training for PIU staff; red flag detection training; NHA's established contract approving mechanism

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial environmental impacts from land clearing, vegetation loss, soil erosion, landscape transformation in arid environment; blasting and excavation impacts; water availability changes; air and noise pollution; physical cultural resources (PCRs) at risk; displacement of approximately 4,300 people; labor influx of 500-700 workers creating SEA risks; potential for sex trafficking around labor camps and transport terminals; HIV transmission risks; damage to utilities during construction; increased traffic-related impacts under Component II
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) and Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) prepared for Component I; Environmental Management Framework (EMF) for Component II; Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) for Component I; Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) and Social Management Framework (SMF) for Component II; avoidance of unnecessary vegetation clearance; maintenance of natural drainage; water wastage reduction guidelines; proper blasting procedures; labor influx management plan; SEA risk mapping and mitigation; local organization with GBV expertise hired; service providers identified for SEA survivors; multi-tier Grievance Redress Mechanism with SEA protocols; Codes of Conduct with SEA prohibitions; mandatory training for contractors and security personnel on GBV/SEA; gender-sensitive amenities in economic clusters; afforestation along PTEX; climate-resilient design; third-party environmental monitoring; independent RAP implementation monitoring; Engineer enforcement of contractual clauses including water supply provision if pipes damaged

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential community resistance; limited local capacity to manage labor influx; acceptance and under-reporting of GBV in communities; restricted mobility and social norms affecting women's participation; low literacy levels; weak property rights
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Consultative process for ESIA, RAP, EMF, RPF, SMF preparation including focus group discussions and village-level public meetings; multi-tier GRM accessible to all stakeholders; gender-responsive and culturally appropriate grievance mechanisms; involvement of Jirgas and local tribes; community engagement on SEA risks and legal rights; awareness campaigns on GBV/SEA; target of 10% women-owned/managed firms benefiting from Component II; gender-sensitive transportation and workplace infrastructure; complementary World Bank Women Entrepreneurs Finance Initiative (WeFi) exploration; merit-based appointment of women in project-associated agencies; dissemination of job opportunities to women communities

identified_risks.risk_description: Large single D&B contract (US$ 362 million) for which NHA has relatively less experience; complexity of procurement process; potential delays in contract award; need for specialized expertise in D&B contracts
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: NHA's experience with D&B contracts (US$ 5.7 billion in contracts for 700+ km expressways/motorways); positive experience with D&B approach showing better schedule performance than item-rate contracts; experienced international consultants for bid package preparation; World Bank Procurement Regulations compliance; STEP system for transparency; dedicated NHA procurement staff for project; periodic Bank procurement training; FIDIC-accredited training in D&B contracts; estimation techniques training for lumpsum contracts; red flag detection training; prequalification documents submitted to Bank for no-objection; contract signed July 2019

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential for use of security forces to create risks; limited access to project area due to security situation; alternative supervision mechanisms needed
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Assessment of security force risks with commensurate mitigation measures; use of Frontier Constabulary with good reputation and no known negative legacy; mandatory training/sensitization on gender issues and GBV; limited interaction expected between FC and community due to mostly uninhabited alignment; innovative supervision measures including GPS-enabled cameras for asset verification; mobile phone-based voice messaging for beneficiary participation; third-party monitoring; six-monthly supervision missions; incident checking and reporting; reassessment of security conditions periodically

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Multi-tiered Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) with three levels: (1) village/jirga level; (2) tehsil/district subdivision level; (3) PIU or Project Steering Committee level. GRM is gender-responsive, culturally appropriate, readily accessible to PAPs at no cost and without retribution. Includes survivor-centered protocols for SEA complaints with referral mechanisms to service providers. Registers accessible to communities. Interactive Voice Response System being considered. Grievance response time standards to be monitored as indicator.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Large gender gaps identified: female labor force participation in KP 14% (vs 59.4% for males) and FATA 8.6% (vs 56.4% for males) vs national average 25% (vs 83% for males). Female literacy rates: FATA 16.6% (vs 76.3% for males), KP 35% (vs 71% for males). Only 0-4.9% of businesses managed/owned by women. Project targets 10% of firms benefiting from Component II investments to be women-owned/managed. Gender specialist in PIU. Gender-sensitive amenities in economic clusters including safe transport, separate washrooms, prayer rooms, childcare centers, harassment-free workplaces. Women's participation in GRM. Merit-based appointment of women in project-associated agencies. Exploration of complementarities with World Bank Women Entrepreneurs Finance Initiative (WeFi). Activities leveraging cultural heritage targeted to maximize women's participation. Gender analysis and proposed actions detailed in Annex 3.
vulnerable_groups: Business operators (46 owner operators and 143 tenant operators affected)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed to address drivers of fragility through economic development in FATA. Identified drivers of fragility include high unemployment among young men, inadequate livelihoods, and absence of sufficient employment opportunities. Component II expected to stimulate employment and livelihood opportunities through private sector development. Project involves local tribes in transport and trade to help mitigate security risks. Increases in economic activity expected to improve security situation in FATA and eastern Afghanistan. Project contributes to World Bank priority of reducing fragility through economic development. Complementary investments in regional connectivity (CPEC, CASA-1000, TAP-I pipeline) ushering in new era of cooperation. Project leverages bilateral institutional mechanisms (JEC, JBC) to promote regional cooperation and policy harmonization.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: FATA is fragile and conflict-affected with 73% of population in multidimensional poverty (vs 43% in KP, 39% nationally). Over recent decades, border disputes, diplomatic tensions, successive waves of conflict, and dilapidated infrastructure have stifled development. Security situation has improved: insurgent attacks fell 50% in 2015, 28% further drop in 2016, though increased in first half 2017 before dropping in second half. Constraints on trade through Khyber Pass have hindered development of Afghanistan, Pakistan, and Central Asia and undermined stability of northwestern Pakistan. Trade between Pakistan and CARs minimal (less than 1% of CAR imports). Trade between Afghanistan and Pakistan dropped 30% over past 2 years. FATA merger with KP planned over 5 years to remove regulatory differences discouraging investment. Post-conflict reconstruction ongoing with improved security enabling infrastructure investments.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security remains a risk that could impact implementation despite recent improvements. Project involves use of Frontier Constabulary (FC) - well-trained federal paramilitary police force with leadership from Civil Armed Forces and no known negative legacy issues - to provide safe working environment during design and construction. FC personnel from the area will receive mandatory training/sensitization on gender issues including GBV and SEA in context of Pakistan Penal Code and relevant national/provincial laws. Criminal offences by FC prosecuted under Pakistan Penal Code 1860 (Amended 2006) with elaborated provisions on protection of women and children. Limited interaction expected between FC and community due to mostly uninhabited alignment of PTEX. Bank team will assess security risks during supervision and ensure reporting of incidents/complaints. Alternative supervision mechanisms developed due to UN Phase III security risk level for KP.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Project will significantly impact approximately 4,300 people (500 households) who will permanently lose land, houses, trees, crops and/or other non-movable assets. Of these, 1,400 persons will self-relocate to individual sites/structures of their own choice. Land acquisition: approximately 1,193 acres total (137 acres individually owned, 1,055 acres collectively owned by clans/khels, 1 acre public land). Public infrastructure to be rebuilt/relocated: 2 mosques, 1 school, 1 hospital, 2 water supply plants, 4 water tanks, 4 pylons, 163 electric poles, 3 dug wells. Business impacts: 189 business operators affected (46 owner operators, 143 tenant operators), 72 employees losing income. Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) prepared for Component I with entitlement matrix. Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) and Social Management Framework (SMF) prepared for Component II. Full compensation and relocation assistance required prior to displacement per World Bank policy. RAP implementation monitored by independent agency with third-party validation. Resettlement Coordination Committee established. Monitoring focuses on vulnerable groups including female-headed households and economically backward families. Project also expected to benefit internally-displaced persons through employment opportunities.
